title: Kill-switch has no effect: promo banner still served hours after disabling the flag
body:
### Bug report

We flipped `promo_banner` **off** in FlagPilot at 09:40 UTC today because the promo pricing in the banner was wrong. The FlagPilot dashboard confirms the flag is disabled and their API returns `false` when we curl it directly.

Three hours later, production `/api/config` still returns:

```json
{ "promo_banner": true, "maintenance_mode": false, "new_nav": false }
```

Rolling-restarting the pods makes the new value show up immediately, which is how we eventually killed the banner. So the toggle itself works — it just never reaches the config service until a restart. Is FlagPilot's edge API caching stale values for our server-side key, or are we holding it wrong?

This was supposed to be our emergency kill-switch. An emergency switch that needs a redeploy to act is not a kill-switch.

title: /api/config returns different flag values per pod for hours after a toggle
body:
### Bug report

After toggling `new_nav` on yesterday, `/api/config` started returning inconsistent values depending on which pod answers: pods restarted after the toggle serve `new_nav: true`, older pods keep serving `false`. It converged only after the evening deploy recycled everything.

```
$ for i in $(seq 1 6); do curl -s https://config.acme.dev/api/config | jq .new_nav; done
false
true
false
false
true
false
```

Clients behind the same LB flap between old and new nav on every request. We assumed the LB was caching, but response headers show `cache-control: no-store` and distinct pod ids. Whatever caches flag values inside the service doesn't seem to expire until the process dies.
